text:p.text:span: 57 pl. 1 [photographs only]). New photographs kindly provided by Dr. Shahrokh Razmjou and

office:text.text:p: ANALYTICAL RECONSTRUCTION OF TEXT IN STANDARD SUMERIAN ORTHOGRAPHY AND AKKADIAN TRANSCRIPTION

text:p.text:span: You make me very happy with the news that you sent me.
Who could give me a house-born slave as reliable as you? ­
Who has someone so able entrusted to his lord?
Now, concerning all that you have written to me,
I am sending you a tablet with the secret instructions.
Babati, the high commissioner, who is (like) a grandfather to me,
the judicious elder, skilled in council,
has now arrived before you (with)­ six hundred talents of silver and six hundred talents of gold.
I have (also) sent you news about the troops: forward them to him
Please be careful ­that his heart does not turn (against you)!
And as for ­you, take the gold and silver from his care,
and buy all the grain at the prevailing market rate.
Once it is all in your possession, you do not have to hurry
From this day on you are ­the son who gladdens my heart.
You are established (as the guardian) against all of Amurru and Elam; sit as my representative!
Sit before them on a throne (set up on) a gold (encrusted) dais,
so that you will be the one before whom their envoys prostrate themselves.
Elevate yourself over them, never turn back!
As for a soldier who finds favor­ with you,
build for him his (first) adult house---your favor will be great.
Now, concerning all that I have­ written to you,
it is urgent, you must not change your allegiance!
I greatly rejoiced at the news that you sent me.
Who­ could give me a servant who is as reliable as you?
Who has one so ­able who is so exceedingly pleasing to his lord?
Now, concerning all that you have written to me,
I have now sent to you Babati, the high commissioner,
the judicious elder, who is skilled in council, (together with) six hundred talents of silver and sixty talents of­ gold;
he has come to you in the matter of the credit balance left over from what the troops have already received
News of my troops have been sent to you:
forward them to him and do whatever he asks.
Please be careful that his ­heart does not turn against you!
And as for you, take ­the gold and silver from his hand and buy up the all grain at the­ prevailing market rate,
and should anyone claim it from you, you should pay it no mind. ­
From this day on, you are the son who gladdens my heart.
You are established (as the guardian) against all of Amurru ­and Elam; sit as my representative!
You sit before them­ on a throne (set up on) a gold (encrusted) dais,
so that you will be the one before whom their envoys prostrate themselves.
Elevate yourself over them, so that you never turn back!
As for a soldier who finds favor with you,­
build for him his (first) adult house so that he will return your­ favor.
Now, concerning all that you have written to me,
it is urgent, do not change your allegiance!

One must keep in mind that this letter is undoubtedly a late Old or­ Middle Babylonian pastiche of the Shulgi correspondence and that the one existing manuscript is post-OB; we have no idea of when and where it was written, or whether the Susa characteristics are
primary or secondary. The transliteration follows closely the edition of Edzard but has been collated on photographs. The line numbers in the commentary refer to the analytical reconstruction.
. As is the case with all known bilingual letters, except for the
, the Akkadian does not render the opening formula.
is an awkward attempt to render the adverbial form

; this suggests that the author either composed the text in Akkadian, and then translated it into Sumerian, or that he was thinking in terms of Akkadian when he concocted the letter. Note that adverbials in -
are more characteristic of later stages of Standard Babylonian (e.g., W. Meyer,
64 [1995] 161–86]); only five such occurrences are known from OB literary texts (N. Wasserman,
132–33). Nevertheless, this expression harkens back to the use of
on OAkk letters (B. Kienast and K. Volk,
. Confusions abound here. The Akkadian has the rare
, which was clearly intended as elative; the Sumerian has
in Sumerian but it is possible that the scribe confused
on the Sumerian verb cannot be the quotative, nor should it be a plural ending, and therefore is most likely a mistake, driven by the –
. Here, once again there are problems with the translation ­of the verb
. The Sumerian has “I have written to you,”­ whereas the Akkadian has “you have written to me.” The same is ­true of line 29. In both cases logic requires that one prefer­ the Sumerian version.
. Once again someone was translating back from Akkadian, albeit imperfectly. The scribe may have confused Akkadian
, a mistake conditioned by the rendering of Sumerian /d/ by /g/ in this text, and then understood the Sumerian as a form of
clearly has nothing to do with

,­ which is entirely missing in the Akkadian. Since Babati was the ­brother of Abi-simti, who was the wife of Åulgi, one would not expect this to be a kinship term. Anything is possible in this ­text but most probably this is a metaphorical expression of ­respect for his age; it unlikely that the author ­knew that Babati was
, “queen’s brother,” and incorrectly deduced this relationship.
. Here the composer used two rare Akkadian words that are otherwise known only from late sources:
Not having a handy Sumerian correspondence for either, he drew upon
, hence the awkward repetition of the same word in the Sumerian version. The use of
is attested in two other late bilinguals but not in any lexical text; see
but the measure should not precede the number. Edzard has already commented on the absurd nature of the numbers. Note the different verbs in the two versions, as well as the Assyrian/SB form
. The Sumerian is a garbled back-translation from Akkadian. Someone mistook
, “without heir,” a rare learned loan word from Sumerian.
. Note the very Akkadian use of
, which is otherwise never used in this manner but is only attested in letter closure formulae. The writing
suggests that the scribe thought that the word for “water” in Sumerian was /
/, as is traditionally transliterated today, and he may have been correct; how he analyzed the etymology of the
. I do not understand either version very well. The Sumerian
åakanka za-e dabﬁ-bé (or dabﬁ-dabﬁ-bé; åakanka zú-e da-da-be-e)
, if properly reconstructed, does not correspond to Akkadian
one expects something on the order of
for the second person bound pronoun, it is also possible that he misinterpreted
) does not in any way render Akkadian
It is possible that the author confused
. Most probably the whole line was garbled as a result of a mix-up between
(“to hurry”) as well as their Sumerian equivalents
, “I never rush (with my decisions) but search out the background.”
, although the evidence for this has never been clear. The lexical lists agree on the Akkadian equivalent
but not on the reading. According to MB Diri the reading should be
15 82]). The later version offers two readings:
is problematical. ­Edzard suggested a connection with Eridu but that does not recommend itself. ­A more probable explanation may be that
. The use of /r/ for /g/ is found often in this tablet.
. I have no explanation for the sign sequence
between the Sumerian and Akkadian versions.
énsi nam-énsi-ta lú Ñárza Ñárza-ta ní-te-ní-te-a li-bí-ib-Ñar ù nu-ub-ta-gub-bu lú nu-un-gaz igi nu-un-hul

has been discussed by ­Edzard in his commentary; I would be more reluctant to use this­ text as evidence for the reading of the Sumerian word for “general.” The matter is complex but it is more likely that the proper reading was
, 549). In this tablet k = d and r = g, hence this is simply a writing for
. Something is very wrong with both the Sumerian and the­ Akkadian here. Edzard struggled with the passage; using some of the same facts I propose a slightly different

interpretation. It seems that whatever the “original” may have contained, some misinterpretation came from a form that had syllabic Sumerian, and the Akkadian version was either composed after the corruption, or was altered to match the new reading. The model for ­this line must be sought in the royal prerogative for execution­ and blinding that is expressed in two other letters: ÅAr1: 23 (2):
, above). One can assume that the “original” text had a­ syllabic version of
, “execute people, blind them.” Everything went wrong; someone reinterpreted the first part of the line as
, which was interpreted as a standing for
, which the lexical texts render as
, “transgressor, guilty person.” The original translation of
on the basis of lexical equivalencies with Akk.
, a verb, or verbs, that still present some interpretive difficulties (see the dictionaries); e.g.

17 224]). Whatever the line of errors, one can be fairly sure­ that at one point the meaning of the line was “You are permitted ­to execute and blind people.” This series of distortions may have also been affected by the association of
, “manliness, maturity,” and it is probable that this is what the author had in mind. The
on the Sumerian word is superfluous.
. The final verbal phrase probably results from a misinterpretation of the Akkadian use of
idiom means “to change allegiance/mood,” and is similar in meaning to
